data.title: Base creator admits sharing ‘Base is for pimping’ art was a mistake
data.description:
The creator of Ethereum layer 2 blockchain Base, Jesse Pollak, has apologized following backlash over posting digital artwork that controversially played on Base’s tagline, “Base is for everyone.”

Several social media users found the artwork offensive and inappropriate.

“It was a single phrase among many, but I’ll own this was a mistake and apologize,” Pollak said in an April 18 X post referring to his decision to reshare a GIF image that featured the phrase “Base is for...” followed by a rotating sequence of words, including both controversial terms like “pimping” and “squirting,” as well as more neutral ones like “art,” “minting,” and “ideas.”

Pollak says he appreciates “provocative art”

Pollak emphasized that the artwork was made by a creator, not him, and specifically apologized for the image featuring the phrase “Base is for pimping.”

Pollak said that while he wants to support artists building on Base and admits he appreciates “provocative art,” he recognizes the need to be mindful of his shared messages, especially when they appear to come directly from him.

It comes after criticism from several crypto industry participants who took to social media to voice their disappointment over Pollak’s endorsement of the image, calling out the use of the word “pimping.”

Crypto commentator “Kristel” said in an April 18 X post, “so we’re just casually platforming pimping now?” “I get pushing boundaries, but this isn’t it,” she said.

“This isn’t provocative and ‘edgy,” she added. Kanto Labs founder said it is an “absolute PR nightmare.”

Meanwhile, crypto commentator David Z. Morris said this “doesn’t just hurt Base, it hurts crypto.” Morris added:

“The specific allusion to sex trafficking (not “sex work,” pimping is pretty fundamentally exploitation) is specifically bad for a sector that needs to advance the narrative that open finance is a net social positive.”

However, many praised Pollak for the apology and his continued efforts to push boundaries in the crypto industry. “Love the honesty. We all make mistakes, but it’s about how we grow from them,” crypto commentator Zuri said.

Bankless co-founder David Hoffman said, “I respect the leadership here.” Milk Road co-founder Kyle Reidhead said, “Do and share whatever you want without apology.”

Base was at the center of controversy only days ago when the official X account shared a post promoting a memecoin with its marketing tagline, “Base is for everyone.” 

It also shared a link to a token of the same name on Zora, a social network where users can make posts into tokens for others to speculate on.

In just over an hour after it was created, the Base is for everyone token hit a peak market capitalization of $17.1 million — then dropped by nearly 90% over the next 20 minutes to a market value of $1.9 million, according to DEX Screener data.

A Coinbase spokeswoman distanced Base from the token, telling Cointelegraph on April 17, "Base did not launch a token.” “This is not an official Base token, and Base did not sell this token. Base posted on Zora, which automatically tokenizes content,” the spokeswoman said.

data.title: Foreign investors increase Chinese bond holdings by $37 bln this year - Xinhua
data.description:
Foreign institutions have increased holdings in China's onshore bonds by more than 270 billion yuan ($36.99 billion) so far this year, highlighting the appeal of the world's second-biggest bond market, the official Xinhua News Agency reported on Saturday.

Foreign institutional holdings in Chinese bonds totalled 4.5 trillion yuan as of April 15, Xinhua reported, citing central bank data.

But foreigners account for just around 2.4% of China's $25 trillion bond market, suggesting a big potential to grow, according to the article.

The People's Bank of China will unswervingly promote bond market opening, and attract more foreign investors, as ties deepen between regions such as the Middle East, Latin America, Europe and Africa, Xinhua said.

China's efforts to lure foreign investment comes as U.S. President Donald Trump's global tariffs threaten to dent investor confidence in U.S. assets.

Prices of U.S. Treasuries and the dollar index are down sharply in recent weeks, driven by concerns about recession and growing grievance against Trump's tariff and diplomatic policies.

($1 = 7.2835 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Correction to Hollywood's Super Networker Article on April 18
data.description: Khaldoon Al Mubarak, head of Abu Dhabi's Mubadala sovereign-wealth fund, who partnered with TKO on bringing the UFC to the United Arab Emirates, praised Emanuel for having "one of the sharpest minds in the industry" but is always a little wary when he invites his friend over to dinner. "Hollywood's Super Networker Is Building an Entertainment Powerhouse" at 8 p.m. ET on April 18 misspelled his name as Khaldoon Al Murbarak.

data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, HDPE film grade prices displayed a mixed undertone in the Asian region. Despite the International Energy Agency (IEA) lowering its global oil demand growth projections, the bearish effect on crude oil prices has been minimal. This is largely due to a decrease in worries regarding potential trade conflicts, following the U.S. decision to implement a 90-day suspension on increased tariff rates. In China, the stability of the US dollar's market price is significantly affected by the diminishing arbitrage opportunities between domestic and international markets. This situation has arisen due to the concurrent decrease in domestic RMB-denominated prices and the strengthening of the exchange rate, which has effectively closed most avenues for imports. As a result of the robust RMB exchange rate and ongoing weakness in the domestic market, importers are exercising increased caution when placing new orders. In the near future, market participants will closely monitor foreign price quotations and the rate of import arrivals at Chinese ports, as these factors could influence short-term market sentiment. With domestic prices facing downward pressure and the RMB struggling to gain momentum, foreign sellers have become less willing to submit competitive bids. This reduced engagement has led to a significant decline in recorded market transactions. Therefore, it will be crucial to observe the timing and volume of imported goods, as well as the distribution of overseas shipments, to anticipate future market developments. Given the current circumstances, the Chinese dollar-denominated market is expected to maintain price stability in the short term. On the supply front, the activation of additional production units is contributing to an increase in supply levels. However, despite a gradual decrease in social inventory, downstream demand for raw materials is entering a period of reduced activity. This mismatch characterized by rising supply and sluggish demand continues to exert pressure on the market. Consequently, the polyethylene sector is anticipated to remain in a weak and volatile condition in the near future. On April 9, the United States enacted reciprocal tariffs, establishing a 10% baseline for most countries for a duration of 90 days, with the exception of China, which faces a 125% tariff. As of 2025, the total US tariffs have reached 145%. In response, China raised tariffs on US goods from 84% to 125%, effective April 12. The escalating trade tensions have dampened market sentiment, leading Chinese polyethylene (PE) buyers to refrain from making purchases despite the availability of ample supply from the Middle East and the coming up of new domestic capacity anticipated to compensate for the decline in imports from the United States. In China, the domestic market remained largely inactive as most participants were busy attending the Chinaplas 2025 event. In Southeast Asia, the polyethylene import market experienced a period of stability this week, as ongoing uncertainty regarding US tariffs continued to cloud the situation. Discussions have emerged suggesting that certain countries in the region may consider reducing tariffs on US products while exploring methods to decrease their trade surpluses with the United States. This has fostered a somewhat more optimistic atmosphere in markets such as Vietnam, Thailand, and Indonesia, which have been grappling with some of the highest tariffs; however, it has not yet resulted in any significant changes in pricing. Offer levels, particularly for shipments from the Middle East, remained largely unchanged. In Vietnam, the emergence of lower-priced offers from the US and China began to exert some pressure on purchasing sentiment. Overall, trading activity was relatively subdued, with most pricing for the week being influenced by a limited number of transactions and general buy-sell interest. In contrast, the market in the Philippines did not align with the regional trend, maintaining a firm stance, with transactions occurring at the higher end of the price spectrum. In India, the demand for polyethylene (PE) grades remained low throughout the week, primarily due to anticipated reductions in offers linked to declining upstream crude oil prices. Suppliers adopted a cautious approach, refraining from active participation as they assessed market sentiment and the potential implications of US trade tariffs. This prudent strategy resulted in limited transactions and muted discussions. Additionally, the presence of substantial inventories at processing facilities, coupled with a slowdown in business activity at the onset of the new financial year, led many buyers to postpone their purchases, further constraining market dynamics. There are a few scheduled turnarounds that may provide some upward momentum for PE prices in the region. However, the extent of this impact remains uncertain, given the significant supply available from various other sources. In Pakistan, the decision by the U.S. government to postpone tariff increases on imports has been positively received by local converters. However, the general market sentiment remains negative, influenced by persistent political instability and the depreciation of the local currency against the U.S. dollar. Nevertheless, in the Pakistan and Bangladesh markets, some increase is foreseen in restocking activities with the approaching Eid ul-Adha festival in early June. In Sri Lanka, the recent decision by the U.S. government to delay tariff hikes on imports has been appreciated by local converters. Nevertheless, the overall market outlook remains pessimistic due to persistent political instability and the depreciation of the local currency against the U.S. dollar. Purchasing activity was also subdued due to a national holiday in the region. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices down adjusted in the Asian region. Despite the International Energy Agency (IEA) lowering its global oil demand growth projections, the bearish effect on crude oil prices has been minimal. This is largely due to a decrease in worries regarding potential trade conflicts, following the U.S. decision to implement a 90-day suspension on increased tariff rates. The decline in prices for polypropylene (PP) was primarily attributed to reduced import offers from international suppliers and a lack of strong purchasing interest in the region. In China, the polypropylene market is currently facing considerable overcapacity, exacerbated by a decline in new orders from downstream industries. There has been a noticeable decrease in interest for international orders, which has further stalled market prices. While it is more probable that polypropylene prices will decline, they are encountering obstacles to any potential increase, leading to increased caution within the industry and a growing bearish outlook. A prevailing atmosphere of risk aversion characterizes the polypropylene market, as the effects of tariff policies have resulted in a significant reduction in overseas inquiries and a marked decline in export activities. As a result, overall downstream demand remains weak, prompting terminal factories to take a more cautious stance on procurement. This situation has worsened the existing supply-demand imbalance. On the demand front, there has been no notable improvement, with the turnover of industrial chain inventories remaining sluggish. The decrease in overseas inquiries, influenced by tariff policies, has further affected export transactions, complicating resource consumption. Currently, weak demand is the primary driver of fluctuations in polypropylene prices. In the Chinese domestic market, buying activity was subdued as the majority of market participants were occupied with ChinaPlas 2025. In Southeast Asia, polypropylene (PP) prices have experienced a slight decline. Some suppliers have begun to offer May shipments at minor discounts due to a notable decrease in buyer demand. Regional producers had anticipated improved profit margins in April, driven by lower feedstock costs, such as naphtha. However, buyers are likely to advocate for reduced prices as well, particularly in light of the ongoing tariff situation. The new tariffs imposed by the Trump administration on Chinese imports to the United States, effective April 9, have raised concerns about diminished demand in the Asia-Pacific region. Additionally, market participants are closely monitoring PP price trends in China, as demand may also decrease there. In Malaysia, PRefChem, a major player in the petrochemical sector, is expected to resume partial operations at its refinery in May. Their No.2 PP plant, with a capacity of 450,000 tons per year, is scheduled to restart, while the No.1 plant, also with a capacity of 450,000 tons per year, has been intermittently operational since February. These additional volumes are likely to contribute to the regional supply in the near future. In India, market participants adopted a cautious approach this week following the introduction of significant new tariffs by the United States. Purchasing activity diminished, as many opted to remain on the sidelines to assess the situation before committing to transactions. There is a widespread anticipation that prices may decline due to the prevailing uncertainty surrounding the reciprocal tariffs. Additionally, downstream demand is experiencing pressure from the newly imposed levies, particularly within the polymers sector. Spot demand is projected to remain subdued this month, with limited potential for recovery. In India, prices for PP homo-grade have decreased due to tepid demand and softer pricing trends. Buyers are postponing purchases in hopes of securing better deals in light of falling crude oil prices and escalating concerns regarding global trade tensions. Conversely, prices for PP block co-polymer have remained stable, primarily due to a reduction in offers and tighter supply conditions. The recent US tariffs on Chinese imports are prompting concerns about their broader economic implications. One potential consequence could be an increase in PP exports from China to markets such as India, which may negatively affect local market sentiment. However, a domestic plant shutdown is anticipated soon, which could restrict local supply and sustain import demand, thereby mitigating some of the adverse market sentiments. In Pakistan, the recent decision by the U.S. government to postpone tariff increases on imports has been positively received by local converters. However, the general market sentiment remains negative. Nevertheless, in the Pakistan and Bangladesh markets, restocking activities are expected to pick up pace with the approaching Eid ul-Adha festival in early June. In Sri Lanka, the decision by the U.S. to delay tariff hikes on imports has been positively received by domestic converters. Nevertheless, the general market outlook remains pessimistic, primarily due to persistent political instability. Additionally, purchasing activity was subdued because of a national holiday in the region. In Bangladesh, the recent choice by the U.S. government to delay tariff hikes on imports has been positively welcomed by domestic converters. Nevertheless, the general market outlook continues to be pessimistic, largely attributed to persistent political instability. Conversely, with Eid ul-Adha approaching in early June, restocking activities are likely to pick up. Market players are optimistic that prices may decline further as a result of ongoing trade tensions. This hesitation could result in lower sales and accumulation of inventory for sellers. PS: Polystyrene prices continued to be steady in Asia this week. Despite the International Energy Agency (IEA) lowering its global oil demand growth projections, the bearish effect on crude oil prices has been minimal. This is largely due to a decrease in worries regarding potential trade conflicts, following the U.S. decision to implement a 90-day suspension on increased tariff rates. Some market participants in Taiwan have indicated that General Purpose Polystyrene (GPPS) is utilized in packaging and disposable cutlery, suggesting that its demand will remain relatively stable. In contrast, High Impact Polystyrene (HIPS), which is utilized in the production of toys and home appliances, is expected to be adversely affected by tariffs. Additionally, the potential for a global economic downturn is likely to influence consumer spending, further diminishing the demand for HIPS. In the first quarter of 2025, production of home appliances was robust, largely due to heightened export activity stemming from tariff-related concerns. However, a decrease in production is projected for the second quarter. The repercussions of the US 'Liberation Day' tariff, along with increasing systemic risks in global trade, have significantly influenced the sentiment in the polystyrene (PS) market this week. Growing fears of a recession have negatively impacted the performance of global risk assets and commodities, including Styrene Monomer (SM), which is a crucial feedstock for PS. Spot demand has largely diminished over the week, as downstream PS users and plastic manufacturers expressed concerns regarding potential declines in offtake and inventory pressures. Market participants are primarily awaiting stabilization in the SM market and broader economic sentiment before proceeding with further spot transactions. In China, The supply of domestic PS has been sufficient, and Chinese exporters have been actively engaged in export activities. However, certain regional market participants anticipate a possible decline in import demand from high-end Chinese PS users. As a result, the PS CFR China spot import market may be perceived as a niche market in the near future. In the Chinese domestic market, buying activity was subdued as the majority of market participants were occupied with ChinaPlas 2025. CFR Southeast Asia quotations from local polystyrene (PS) manufacturers exhibited a significant range, reflecting the diverse sales strategies employed by different suppliers. Some suppliers, motivated by the recent outlook for styrene monomer (SM) markets, were willing to make more considerable concessions on PS prices to encourage purchasing interest. The fierce price competition from Chinese export shipments, which were reported to be priced very competitively, further pressured these suppliers' sales, prompting them to reduce spot prices in anticipation of securing additional transactions. Conversely, certain regional PS manufacturers took a more cautious approach, refraining from offering substantial price reductions immediately. Their primary concern was the volatility in the forward SM market, particularly after observing a rebound in the regional SM market towards the end of the week. Additionally, a few suppliers noted that the decline in buying interest was largely influenced by macroeconomic factors, suggesting that spot demand might be price inelastic. They argued that significant price cuts could prove ineffective in boosting spot demand and might instead foster a more pessimistic market sentiment. Consequently, they opted for a more tempered strategy, making only slight adjustments to their pricing. In India, the demand for imports remained subdued, as buyers chose to hold off in anticipation of price stabilization following significant drops in SM and PS prices. Consequently, there were limited discussions, and the presence of lower-priced Chinese offers prompted major regional importers to avoid engaging in conversations about other Asian products. In Bangladesh, Enquiries and discussions regarding Northeast Asian cargoes remained stable, despite the prevailing concerns about an additional 37% US tariffs on top of the existing 15%. Notably, the implementation of the former tariffs was suspended for three months late this week. While buyers with urgent spot requirements actively sought to secure positions at more advantageous rates, most regional polystyrene (PS) users expressed hesitance in committing to substantial forward positions due to worries about market volatility and uncertainties in end consumption. Market participants largely adopted a wait-and-see approach, anticipating potential tariff negotiations in the coming weeks that could foster a more stable macroeconomic environment and improve global trade prospects. Concurrently, some regional PS manufacturers were managing incoming order cancellations, as the escalating global trade tensions had a tangible impact on the offtakes from downstream PS users. ABS: ABS prices slipped in the Asian region this week. Despite the International Energy Agency (IEA) lowering its global oil demand growth projections, the bearish effect on crude oil prices has been minimal. This is largely due to a decrease in worries regarding potential trade conflicts, following the U.S. decision to implement a 90-day suspension on increased tariff rates. ABS market prices experienced a widespread decline this week. The intensification of the trade war has contributed to a decrease in crude oil and raw material prices. As a result, ABS transactions were sluggish, leading to lower quotations from traders. In terms of raw materials, there was a significant drop in prices of crude oil and other commodities, with styrene prices also declining in tandem. Butadiene prices saw a sharp decrease as well. Consequently, the cost support for ABS weakened, resulting in a continued price drop. Traders exhibited a pessimistic outlook, primarily selling to secure profits. Downstream buyers have retreated from the market, opting to observe rather than engage. Overall market transactions were lacklustre, influenced by negative external factors that drove prices down, with no significant positive support in sight. The poor transaction activity further intensified pressure on the supply side, leading to a steep market decline. Additionally, the persistent weak demand for ABS has created an oversupply situation, prompting producers to either reduce their production rates or temporarily shut down their facilities. The current China-US tariff negotiations are fraught with uncertainty, leaving the future outlook ambiguous for now. Nevertheless, from a broader supply and demand perspective regarding ABS, the oversupply situation appears challenging to rectify. Yulong is anticipated to introduce new products next week, which will likely keep the ABS industry's supply levels elevated. Given these negative influences, it is expected that ABS prices will continue to decline in the short term rather than increase. PVC: This week, PVC prices were left unchanged across the Asian region. Despite the International Energy Agency (IEA) lowering its global oil demand growth projections, the bearish effect on crude oil prices has been minimal. This is largely due to a decrease in worries regarding potential trade conflicts, following the U.S. decision to implement a 90-day suspension on increased tariff rates. PVC spot trading in Asia experienced a slowdown this week as buyers postponed their purchases, anticipating a decline in prices influenced by decreasing crude oil rates and a bleak economic forecast amid ongoing trade disputes. The supply situation in China has improved, yet suppliers are exercising caution as they evaluate the repercussions of US tariffs. The reduction in crude oil prices, coupled with diminished demand for end products in China, could alter PVC trade dynamics. In the ASEAN region, particularly in Vietnam, buyers expressed concern over the proposed 46% US tariff, even with a 90-day postponement, while a 10% baseline tariff has already been implemented since April 9. Despite the decrease in feedstock costs, chlor-alkali manufacturers are still grappling with narrow profit margins due to low caustic soda prices. In China, PVC prices have largely remained stable, although there has been a week-on-week decline in offers for both carbide- and ethylene-based PVC, attributed to a weaker yuan and reduced export demand. The imposition of high tariffs by the US (145%) and China's corresponding tariffs (125%) has diminished prospects for a recovery in demand, particularly in light of the persistent weakness in China's property market. Export activities have slowed as buyers have taken a more cautious approach amid ongoing trade tensions. A slight decrease in supply occurred due to maintenance shutdowns, which aided export deliveries and contributed to a reduction in industry inventories. Nevertheless, domestic trade has remained steady. Prices for upstream feedstocks, including calcium carbide and ethylene, have experienced a slight decline due to improved supply, further diminishing the cost support for PVC production. In summary, uncertainties in foreign trade and policy risks continue to hinder export expectations. In the Chinese domestic market, buying activity was subdued as the majority of market participants were occupied with ChinaPlas 2025. In Southeast Asia, PVC prices remained stable this week, influenced by a lack of activity in the import market, which is attributed to weak demand and ongoing discussions regarding US tariffs. Chinese s-PVC continues to lead in imports, while other exporters are anticipated to announce their May offers shortly; however, potential delays may arise due to trade uncertainties. Furthermore, regional supply is expected to increase as new ethylene-based PVC production capacities in China are scheduled to come on stream by the end of April. In India, PVC prices have remained steady this week, reflecting sluggish demand growth and uncertainty stemming from ongoing tariff and anti-dumping duty (ADD) negotiations. The market is primarily influenced by Chinese s-PVC offers, with other exporters likely to present their May shipment offers after Chinaplas, although delays are probable due to US-China tariff tensions. Buyer engagement has been limited, as most sellers have already fulfilled their April quotas. Nevertheless, there is a growing expectation for stronger import demand for May shipments, driven by domestic supply shortages, reduced inventories, and anticipated lower Chinese FOB prices. However, restocking efforts may remain constrained until there is clarity on the final ADD rulings expected by the end of April. In Pakistan, prices have remained unchanged over the week due to a decelerated trading momentum. Although a few offers surfaced, buyer appetite remained tepid amid an uncertain economic outlook and crude oil price fluctuations. With sufficient inventories and subdued downstream demand, buyers are expected to delay purchase decisions, hoping for more competitive pricing in the weeks ahead. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, prices remained unchanged due to minimal trading activity through the week. PET: This week, PET prices witnessed a drop in the Asian region. Despite the International Energy Agency (IEA) lowering its projections for global oil demand growth, the bearish effect on crude oil prices has been minimal. This is largely due to a decrease in worries regarding potential trade conflicts, following the U.S. decision to implement a 90-day suspension on increased tariff rates. Asian polyethylene terephthalate (PET) prices have reached their lowest point in several years, primarily due to ongoing trade tensions that have adversely affected market sentiment. Following the announcement of reciprocal tariffs by US President Donald Trump, buying activity in Asia has diminished. This situation raises concerns regarding the potential escalation of trade tensions and its implications for economic activity in the region. Notably, bottle-grade PET has been excluded from the new tariff list. This exclusion is significant as it indicates that import costs for bottle-grade PET are likely to remain stable, which could benefit both manufacturers and consumers in the PET packaging sector by preventing price increases that might arise from higher tariffs. Additionally, the market is witnessing a decline in prices, driven by decreasing upstream crude oil and feedstock costs. As crude oil prices fall, the expenses related to the production of petrochemical products, including those used to manufacture PET, also decrease. This reduction in production costs contributes to lower prices for PET products, exerting further downward pressure on overall market prices. Numerous orders are presently experiencing delays due to concerns regarding tariffs, with a trader from China voicing apprehensions about the possibility of new tariffs being introduced. This trader noted that if prices continue to fall, the likelihood of contract defaults increases significantly. Market participants emphasized that placing orders at this juncture carries considerable risk, as ongoing price declines could result in defaults on agreements. Reflecting the negative sentiment in the physical market, PET futures in China faced substantial selling pressure. Nevertheless, some buyers began to re-enter the market to take advantage of the reduced prices. In the polyester sector in China, the overall sentiment remained pessimistic as decreasing crude oil prices further weighed on an already tepid demand. Additionally, purchasing activity was subdued, as many market participants were occupied with the ChinaPlas 2025 event. Spot trading in China and Southeast Asia has been described as limited, reflecting a deficiency in substantial trading volume. Participants in these markets have predominantly opted to avoid active trading, driven by heightened uncertainty surrounding short-term price fluctuations. This prudent strategy indicates that traders are reluctant to engage in buying or selling activities, probably influenced by current market fluctuations, economic signals, or geopolitical issues that generate uncertainty about upcoming price movements. Consequently, the market is witnessing diminished participation, as traders hold off on making decisions until more definitive signals emerge. Meanwhile, PET manufacturers in Malaysia and Vietnam were preparing for possible export interruptions to Brazil, following the country's initiation of an antidumping investigation into PET imports. This action may open up opportunities for suppliers from alternative regions. Europe HDPE: This week, HDPE prices quoted flat in the European region. European HDPE markets experienced stable pricing this week, although activity remained limited. The market was affected by uncertainties stemming from tariff conflicts and ongoing weak demand for derivatives. Many buyers opted to stay on the sidelines, monitoring market trends before finalizing their monthly agreements. Some sellers reported that buyers were able to negotiate lower prices than those previously offered. The availability of HDPE was largely influenced by speculation, as industry players discussed a possible reduction in May due to restricted imports linked to the tariff issues. Demand fundamentals presented a mixed picture, with traders and distributors reporting a lack of enthusiasm for the material, while domestic producers noted robust order intakes for the month. This situation provided support for producers to maintain strong pricing positions established in March. Most European producers were said to have revised their initial price increase targets after reassessing market fundamentals and the volatility caused by tariff disruptions. Other producers were reported to be considering either maintaining prices at current levels or implementing slight increases from March levels. Overall, activity in the European polyethylene (HDPE) market remained low amid heightened global political uncertainty. This environment contributed to significant volatility in upstream crude oil and naphtha prices, leading market participants to adopt a cautious approach. Traders with shipments arriving from the US have indicated strong pressure to reduce their HDPE prices. Concerns about a potential easing of sentiment in May could influence market attitudes as the next month approaches. On April 10, the European Union temporarily suspended the enforcement of retaliatory tariffs on the United States for a period of 90 days. This decision was made in response to President Donald Trump's announcement of pausing the implementation of additional 20% tariffs on EU imports, while maintaining a baseline tariff of 10%. Prior to this, on April 9, EU member states had approved the introduction of reciprocal tariffs on various US imports. This development provided relief to many converters in the flexible packaging sector, who might have otherwise faced increased costs due to tariffs on US-origin imports. Currently, only HDPE grades remain vulnerable to any future escalation in EU-US trade tensions. However, the overall impact on the European market is expected to be relatively limited, as HDPE imports accounted for approximately 28% of all polyethylene imports from the US to the EU last year. The potential for substituting US-origin imports is greater for HDPE grades, which may alleviate European buyers' concerns regarding supply security. The 90-day suspension has effectively created opportunities for importing polyethylene grades from the US into the European market, coinciding with declining export prices from the US. This decline is particularly notable as US polyethylene volumes are losing market share in China due to an intensifying trade conflict between the two nations. During the latter trading sessions of the week, there was some interest in purchasing US-origin polyethylene for future delivery. However, many European sellers are currently hesitant to increase imports from the US, as they seek stability and clarity regarding future trade policies, especially in light of the recent volatility in tariffs. Additionally, low demand in Europe may further discourage such import opportunities, along with pricing risks associated with fluctuations in upstream feedstock dynamics. The economic outlook is increasingly indicating a higher likelihood of recession this year, driven by rising uncertainties around US tariff imposition. Crude prices have experienced significant fluctuations recently. On April 1, the monthly contract price (MCP) for feedstock ethylene dropped by Euro 55/mt compared to March's MCP. However, recent trends in upstream oil prices suggest that ethylene's MCP for May could see another substantial decline. Many buyers are likely to face restocking requirements in May as they deplete their inventories, especially since polyethylene (PE) shipments from the US were cancelled following the commission's proposal on March 13 to impose additional import duties. This situation could lead to supply shortages for certain PE grades in May, potentially giving some sellers an advantage in pricing. Nevertheless, it is premature to determine whether PE prices will resist the anticipated downward trend in feedstock ethylene's MCP. In the European market, PE supplies are reportedly sufficient. On the production side, most PE plants have been operating at full capacity, as producers are motivated by the short-term opportunity to compensate for the decline in PE imports from the US. However, it remains uncertain whether producers will adjust their strategies in the future. A producer in Central Eastern Europe is initiating a scheduled turnaround at its HDPE plants, which is expected to continue until mid-June. There are initial indications that HDPE prices may be starting to decline in April. Prior to the announcement of a pause on tariff implementation, numerous polyethylene producers aimed to enhance their profit margins by pursuing price stability for the month. Sellers of HDPE imports from the Mideast Gulf attempted to raise prices in anticipation of possible tariffs on US-origin imports; however, this strategy failed to generate significant buyer appetite. LDPE: This week, LDPE spot prices down adjusted in the European region. The European LDPE markets experienced stable to slight declines in spot prices this week, influenced by uncertainties surrounding tariff disputes and ongoing weakness in demand for derivatives. Spot prices decreased as market tightness eased and overall sentiment weakened. While low-density polyethylene (LDPE) is less dependent on imports, the softening sentiment in other grades may have an impact on LDPE as well. Activity in the European polyethylene (PE) market remained low this week, amid rising global political uncertainties. This situation contributed to significant volatility in upstream crude oil and naphtha prices, leading many market participants to adopt a cautious approach. Numerous buyers chose to remain inactive, observing market trends before finalizing their monthly agreements. Some sellers reported that buyers were able to negotiate lower prices than those previously offered. Additionally, the supply of LDPE was noted to be relatively balanced over the past week, despite reduced operating rates from domestic producers. Demand fundamentals showed mixed signals, as traders and distributors reported a lack of enthusiasm for the material, while domestic producers indicated strong order volumes for the month. The European Union had proposed implementing reciprocal tariffs on imports from the United States, with a tentative start date of April 13, pending approval. This proposal specifically targeted polyethylene (PE) imports from the US, with preliminary estimates indicating potential tariffs around 25%. Meanwhile a decision by the EU to put counter-tariffs on hold has mitigated the immediate pricing impact on US-sourced PE shipments that are already on their way to Europe. The complete effects on supply and pricing are anticipated to be more evident by May. In the meantime, the Easter holiday shutdowns at various converter plants across Europe may reduce short-term demand, providing a temporary buffer against market disruptions throughout April. Crude oil prices have experienced significant fluctuations this week. On April 1, the monthly contract price (MCP) for feedstock ethylene decreased by Euro 55/mt as compared to March's MCP. However, recent trends in upstream oil prices suggest that ethylene's MCP for May could face another substantial decline. The negative outlook for feedstock has led to a reduction in demand and increased caution among buyers, who anticipate further decreases in polyethylene (PE) prices. Many are postponing their purchases until May where feasible, opting to make as-required purchases. The ability of buyers to do this largely depends on their existing stock levels, particularly since some had already reduced their procurement in early March due to a similar pricing forecast. While some buyers refrained from restocking in late March, others chose to secure some of their volume needs early in April to mitigate potential supply risks. Market participants have also noted weaker-than-expected demand across various value chains, which has diminished converters' urgency to replenish their inventories, especially with the upcoming Easter holiday closures affecting many plants. Additionally, contract negotiations are expected to shift focus, as discussions around tariffs have been temporarily sidelined. Current negotiations range from maintaining prices to implementing double-digit increases, despite the decline in monomer prices for April. This situation provides producers with leverage to uphold strong pricing strategies established in March. Many European producers have reportedly revised their initial price increase targets downward after reassessing market fundamentals and the volatility caused by tariff issues. Other producers are positioned between maintaining current prices and implementing increases from March levels. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices rolled over in the European region. The European LLDPE markets experienced stable pricing this week, despite mixed reports regarding supply and a general lack of activity. The market was further impacted by uncertainties stemming from tariff disputes and ongoing weak demand for derivatives. Many buyers opted to remain cautious, monitoring market trends before finalizing their monthly agreements. Some sellers reported that buyers were able to negotiate lower prices than those previously offered over the past week. Feedback on the supply of LLDPE C4 grades in Europe was varied; some sources noted that supply was tightening, while others claimed that stock levels remained sufficient. Demand dynamics were also inconsistent, with traders and distributors reporting a lack of interest in purchasing material, whereas domestic producers indicated strong order volumes for the month. This situation has allowed producers to maintain firm pricing strategies since March. Most European producers have since adjusted their initial price increase targets, reconsidering the market fundamentals and the volatility caused by tariff issues. Other producers were reported to be aiming for price stability or modest increases of up to Euro 50/mt from March levels. Overall, activity in the European polyethylene (PE) market remained low amid rising global political uncertainties, which contributed to significant fluctuations in upstream crude oil and naphtha prices. As a result, market participants have been hesitant to engage. Traders with shipments arriving from the US have already indicated strong pressure to reduce their LLDPE prices. Concerns about a potential easing of sentiment in May could influence market attitudes as the next month approaches. On April 10, the European Union temporarily suspended the enforcement of retaliatory tariffs on the United States for a period of 90 days. This decision was made in response to President Donald Trump's announcement the previous day, which also involved a pause on the implementation of additional 20% tariffs on EU imports, although a baseline tariff of 10% remains in effect. Prior to this announcement, on April 9, EU member states had approved the introduction of reciprocal tariffs on a range of US imports. According to a draft list of products, most LLDPE imports would be exempt from these retaliatory tariffs if the EU proceeds with countermeasures against the US. The European Commission has not yet released the final list of US products that would face any countermeasures; however, before Trump's unexpected announcement, HS code 39014000 was removed from consideration. Other HS codes related to various PE grades were included in the draft list and are proposed to be subject to 25% tariffs. The timeline for the potential implementation of EU tariffs remains uncertain. This development occurs as Europe continues to rely on LLDPE imports from the US for certain grades, including LLDPE butene and metallocene LLDPE. Additionally, the UK has also excluded US-origin LLDPE imports under HS code 390140 from its provisional list of products that may be subject to reciprocal tariffs. The 90-day suspension has effectively created opportunities for import arbitrage of LLDPE grades from the United States into the European market. This situation is further exacerbated by declining export prices from the US, particularly as these LLDPE volumes are losing ground in the Chinese market due to a significant intensification of the trade conflict between the two nations. During the latter trading sessions of the week, some interest was noted for US-sourced LLDPE imports for future delivery. However, many European sellers are currently hesitant to increase imports from the US, as they seek greater stability and clarity regarding future trade policies, especially in light of the potential for abrupt changes in tariffs, as witnessed this week. Additionally, weak demand in Europe may serve as another obstacle to pursuing such arbitrage opportunities, along with pricing uncertainties arising from fluctuations in upstream feedstock markets. The economic outlook is now indicating a greater likelihood of a recession this year, driven by increased uncertainty surrounding US tariffs. Crude oil prices have experienced significant fluctuations this week. On April 1, the monthly contract price (MCP) for feedstock ethylene recorded a decrease of Euro 55/mt compared to the March MCP. However, recent trends in upstream oil prices suggest a further substantial decline for ethylene's MCP in May. Many buyers are likely to encounter restocking requirements in May as they deplete their inventories, particularly since polyethylene (PE) shipments from the United States were canceled following the commission's proposal on March 13 to impose additional import duties. This situation may lead to supply shortages for certain PE grades in May, potentially granting some pricing power to sellers. Nevertheless, it is premature to determine whether PE prices will resist the anticipated downward trend in feedstock ethylene's MCP. In the European market, PE supplies are reportedly sufficient. Operationally, most PE plants have been running at full capacity, as producers are motivated by the short-term opportunity to compensate for the decline in PE imports from the US. However, it remains uncertain whether producers will adjust their strategies moving forward. In the meantime, there are early indications of a decline in PE prices in April. Prior to the announcement of a pause on tariff implementation, many PE producers aimed to enhance their margins by pursuing price stability in April. Some sellers also expressed intentions to increase prices for linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE) grades, with initial settlements for April reflecting price stability for these grades. PP: This week, PP injection spot prices slipped, while PP spot Block Copolymer and Contract remained steady in the European region. The European polypropylene market experienced a decline in prices for injection-grade polypropylene this week, driven by ongoing weak demand and limited market activity. The presence of abundant, lower-cost imports also contributed to downward pressure on prices. Spot prices for polypropylene continued to drop as numerous sellers adjusted their pricing strategies downward. This situation led to significant fluctuations in upstream crude oil and naphtha prices, causing many market participants to adopt a cautious approach. Contrary to earlier expectations that trading activity would increase in April compared to March, the European PP market remained subdued. March had already seen reduced buying activity due to the anticipation that polypropylene prices would decrease in line with falling propylene feedstock prices. The weakening demand for polypropylene is being felt across various sectors, particularly in the automotive industry, where there is low interest in major purchases, as well as in construction and rigid applications. The economic outlook has shifted to indicate a higher likelihood of recession this year, fueled by increased uncertainty surrounding US tariffs. This has undermined confidence among participants in the polypropylene market, leading many to avoid risks associated with long-haul imports from the Middle East and Asia Pacific. In addition to the pricing risks linked to fluctuations in upstream feedstock costs, the combination of low demand and abundant supply in the European market is further discouraging activity. This week, spot material was reported to be readily available, with imported supplies from Asia also on the market. The decline in freight rates from Asia to Europe, coupled with a weakening dollar, has enhanced arbitrage opportunities for South Korean sellers. Additionally, material from the Middle East was noted to be accessible. A significant escalation in the trade conflict between the US and China may impact European polypropylene (PP) pricing dynamics. China's imposition of high tariffs on US imports, including propane, has increased the baseline for PP production costs within China. This situation is anticipated to lead to a decrease in the operating rates of Chinese PP plants, potentially allowing sellers from other Asian nations to concentrate more on supplying the Chinese market. While this could provide some relief for European producers, it remains uncertain whether there will be any shifts in PP demand in China and other Asian markets in the future. Buyers are reportedly delaying their purchases due to a downward trend in upstream naphtha prices, leading to expectations of a reduction in the May propylene monthly contract price (MCP). Market participants are also closely monitoring how tariffs may influence trade flows for polypropylene (PP) and its feedstock. The negative outlook for feedstock has prompted a pullback in demand, causing buyers to exercise increased caution amid predictions of falling PP prices in the near future. Many buyers are postponing their procurement until May when feasible, opting to purchase only as necessary. The ability of buyers to do this largely depends on their existing stock levels, particularly since some had previously reduced their procurement in early March due to a similar pricing forecast for the upcoming month. Additionally, market players have noted weaker-than-anticipated demand across various value chains, which has diminished converters' urgency to replenish their stocks, especially with the upcoming Easter holiday closures at many plants. However, many buyers will likely need to restock in May as their inventories dwindle. This situation, combined with a decrease in imports, may provide some leverage to PP producers in maintaining contract margins, although it is premature to draw definitive conclusions. In April, PP prices have continued to trend downward. Ongoing negotiations for PP contracts indicate that producers are aiming to expand their margins by retaining part of the Euro 55/mt reduction in the April MCP for feedstock propylene. There have been reports of earlier price offers being lowered, but these have not materialized as buyers are advocating for more significant reductions. Commodity grades of PP homopolymer injection and PP block copolymer are reportedly facing greater pressure, while specialized PP grades, except those linked to the automotive sector, may be better positioned to recover some margin or at least avoid margin erosion. Significant price reductions could occur in instances where base prices are elevated or where producers are under pressure to sell specific grades. PS: This week, PS contract prices eased, while spot prices quoted flat in the European region. Negotiations for the April contract prices in the European polystyrene market were significantly influenced by the fluctuations in upstream styrene observed during the week. As a result, PS contract prices experienced a decline, aligning with styrene monomer prices, which decreased by Euro 58/mt compared to the previous month. Demand within the European polystyrene (PS) market remained stable in April, mirroring the conditions of March without any notable changes. However, a slight reduction in activity is anticipated in the latter half of April due to public holidays, including Easter. Projections for demand indicate that overall consumption in 2025 is expected to remain steady in comparison to 2024. Despite the lackluster demand, supply remains balanced to ample, with producers operating at reduced output levels to align with market demand. Furthermore, competitive imports from Asia are anticipated for May deliveries, while freight costs remain low. In the downstream sector, the food packaging industry has shown no significant changes, with no increase in demand for ice cream packaging reported. Although there has been no rise in purchasing interest, the packaging sector continues to bolster the overall polystyrene market. Construction activities have been limited due to prevailing weak economic conditions. Additionally, the strengthening of the US dollar against the euro has affected imports, with exchange rate volatility and delayed deliveries impacting their competitiveness, as most transactions are conducted in US dollars. Currently, two plants in the upstream styrene market, located in Ludwigshafen, Germany, and Moerdijk, the Netherlands, are operating at reduced capacities. There are anticipations of a tightening in styrene supply, along with a potential increase in market volatility in 2026, following the anticipated closure of the Maasvlakte plant in October 2025. Market participants express concerns regarding the possible effects of US tariffs on the supply chain, as the situation remains fluid. At present, there is a 90-day suspension of reciprocal tariffs; however, many stakeholders are apprehensive about making business decisions in light of the short to medium-term implications. The upstream markets for styrene and benzene are expected to be affected by these tariffs, but many uncertainties persist, prompting market players to closely monitor the evolving situation. ABS: This week, ABS prices decreased in the European region. The European Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS) market experienced a downturn this week, driven by weak demand, rising production costs, and supply constraints, which collectively highlight ongoing challenges within the market. The overall sentiment remained pessimistic, with industry sources expressing concerns about the evolving geopolitical and regulatory environment. At the same time, the primary raw materials for ABS, styrene and butadiene, saw price reductions, contributing to downward pressure on ABS prices. Consequently, ABS prices in Europe followed a declining trend amid a bearish market atmosphere. Elevated production levels and an influx of imports from Asia resulted in increased competition within the market. Additionally, sources indicated that the European Commission's antidumping investigation into materials from South Korea and UNV1 has heightened demand for European-produced materials, as market participants are wary of potential retroactive duties if antidumping duties are implemented. However, this uptick in demand has not translated into increased activity in the spot market. It was also noted that while ABS demand is on the rise, buyers of acrylonitrile (ACN) for ABS production are primarily securing contract volumes instead of engaging in the ACN spot market. Furthermore, a supply shortage for flame-retardant ABS has been reported, attributed to China's reduction of antimony trioxide feedstock exports starting in 2024. Downstream demand in the automotive and appliance sectors has shown persistent weakness, with the downstream industry hesitant to place new orders. Additionally, rising raw material costs have significantly contributed to the decline in ABS prices. Overall market sentiment remains subdued, characterized by limited purchasing interest. The European manufacturing sector has seen a slow recovery, with slight improvements noted in March. Despite some growth, new orders remain weak, and industrial activity has not fully rebounded. In the UK, industrial production continues to fall, further exacerbating the decline in ABS demand. Consequently, the ABS market is expected to remain under pressure as supply continues to exceed demand. PVC: This week, PVC contract prices decreased, while spot prices quoted flat in the European region. European spot prices for polyvinyl chloride remained stable over the past week, as sluggish demand eased some of the supply constraints. The European PVC market was predominantly influenced by various regional production challenges, alongside a rise in imports of Asian suspension PVC (s-PVC). Asian spot shipments continue to be available at attractive prices. Traders noted that the arbitrage opportunity for importing from Asia to Europe is still viable. However, consumers have shown increased reluctance to place orders for Asian products due to the decline in European naphtha prices observed in April. Naphtha is a crucial feedstock for ethylene, which is essential for PVC production. In recent weeks, supply in Europe has tightened. A regional manufacturer has declared force majeure on the production of suspension polyvinyl chloride for K70 and K64 grades, with the disruption anticipated to last several weeks at its facility in Tavaux, France. This follows another producer's declaration of force majeure concerning PVC and vinyl chloride monomer at its Marl site in Germany. Additionally, a PVC production unit in Poland faced renewed technical difficulties, leading to the cancellation of April deliveries after previous issues in March. Maintenance work at the PVC production site in Pernis, the Netherlands, has also been extended. Despite the force majeure declarations resulting in reduced supply, the overall situation is not critically tight, as low purchasing interest has mitigated some of the supply-side pressures. Buyer responses have been varied, with many noting the relative ease of finding alternative sources to compensate for the lost supply. Seasonal demand from the construction sector has not yet emerged. Some European participants reported that demand in April has exceeded expectations, contrary to earlier predictions of a slowdown due to the Easter holidays. Market participants noted that there was a general consensus anticipating a less active April because of the holiday, but certain supply disruptions have made the situation more dynamic than initially expected. Contract negotiations are ongoing in the market. Producers have pointed to tight margins as a factor influencing their negotiation strategies. The April contract discussions commenced following a decrease in the ethylene monthly contract price (MCP), with buyers seeking additional discounts due to limited growth in demand. Economic uncertainty continues to impact the market, with recent tariff negotiations in the US heightening concerns about a potential recession, as well as decreased consumer spending and construction activity throughout Europe. Construction demand across Europe remains subdued. Notable declines in construction activity persist in Germany and France. The housing sector continues to significantly affect the index; however, market participants have observed that, despite the muted activity in this sector, it has not yet resulted in a decrease in PVC volumes across the market. PET: This week, PET prices drifted lower in the European region. The European PET resin market has been experiencing a steady decline, primarily due to nd from downstream sectors, reduced production costs, and various economic challenges. During the past week, the spot market exhibited a lack of buying interest, as consumers maintained adequate inventory levels. Additionally, trading activity remained limited following the holiday season. A shift in consumer preferences towards reusable containers has negatively impacted the demand for single-use PET packaging, reflecting changing market dynamics. Furthermore, the rise in imports of lower-cost PET materials from international markets has intensified competitive pressure on local manufacturers. While these imports have bolstered regional consumption, they have simultaneously restricted the pricing power of domestic producers. The downturn in demand from the bottle and food packaging industries, along with a notable decline in PTA and MEG prices, has contributed to the prevailing bearish trend. The market has also been further pressured by oversupply resulting from increased imports and domestic production. Low consumer confidence amid ongoing economic uncertainty has exacerbated the overall weak market sentiment. The market is currently experiencing a significant downturn, as consumers adopt a cautious approach while anticipating further reductions in prices. Producers are operating at reduced capacity due to lower-than-expected demand from preform manufacturers this year. This week, the demand for PET remained weak, as consumers opted to retain their existing stock and hesitated to acquire additional quantities in light of the ongoing decline in prices. The market's downward trend persisted, driven by cautious purchasing practices, declining PTA costs, and falling MEG prices. In addition, the demand for imported materials decreased this week as domestic prices continued to fall, leaving sellers struggling to offload their inventory before the Easter holidays, given the lack of buyer interest. Similarly, in the UK, supply levels remained high, with market participants reporting no difficulties in obtaining materials if needed. USHDPE: This week, HDPE export prices displayed a mixed undertone in the US. HDPE prices exhibited a mixed trend, with discrepancies noted across different grades as the market adapted to the 90-day tariff suspension and an overall negative outlook. Buyers exercised caution, choosing to acquire only quantities they felt assured about selling, amidst ongoing uncertainty regarding the changing tariff situation. This prudent strategy has been consistently observed among distributors since the latter part of the first quarter. Market participants pointed out that inventory replenishment was restricted to replacement levels, with minimal interest in additional stocking. Many buyers continued to maintain high inventory levels accumulated earlier in the year due to fears of potential port strikes and the initial wave of tariff announcements, which have since complicated purchasing decisions. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices were assessed steady in the US. US spot export prices for low density polyethylene (LDPE) held steady during the past week, as demand in the polymer export sector continued to falter amid ongoing tariff uncertainties. Market participants noted a backwardated pricing structure, indicative of the prevailing bearish sentiment that has persisted since the decline in distributor activity observed in March. Furthermore, polyethylene feedstocks faced mounting pricing pressure due to weak demand and high inventory levels throughout the supply chain. These elements combined to create a cautious perspective among polyethylene stakeholders, further solidifying a bearish market sentiment as April advanced. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices quoted higher in the US. In the week that concluded, US spot export prices for LLDPE increased across all grades, sustaining premiums as new orders commenced under the 90-day tariff suspension. However, despite this temporary reprieve, market participants noted a continued lack of robust demand for US-origin polyethylene. Concerns over export delays and extended shipping times persisted, potentially impacting deliveries even during the 90-day period. These logistical challenges, along with shifting tariff conditions, contributed to a cautious and uncertain outlook among buyers in the market. PP: This week, PP export prices were assessed lower in the US. US spot export prices for polypropylene experienced a decline in the past week, as the demand for US-sourced material remained weak, even with the recent reduction in tariff restrictions. The high feedstock costs continued to diminish the competitiveness of US polypropylene in the international market, making it less appealing compared to more affordably priced options from Asia and the Middle East. Market observers pointed out that China has retained a leading role in global polypropylene trade, with a notable rise in the volume of Chinese-origin products entering Latin American markets following the intensification of tariffs between the US and China. This alteration in trade dynamics has further strained US polypropylene exporters, who found it increasingly difficult to establish a foothold in a more competitive global environment. PS: This week, PS export prices slipped sharply in the US. US polystyrene prices experienced varied fluctuations throughout the week, as increases in upstream prices in the US coincided with subdued downstream demand in the Asian market. Export prices for general-grade polystyrene and high-impact polystyrene rose by USD 55/mt, reflecting the upward movement in benzene prices. Nevertheless, the overall market sentiment remained wary, as the diminished demand in Asia somewhat counterbalanced the favorable effects of escalating upstream costs. PVC: This week, PVC export prices down adjusted in the US. The US export PVC market experienced a slowdown in activity during the past week, as the majority of monthly purchases had already been finalized, prompting participants to turn their attention to the forthcoming price nominations for May. At the same time, ongoing concerns regarding tariffs have negatively impacted overall market sentiment. Many market players expect a possible decrease in prices in the upcoming weeks; however, traders have observed that even with reduced prices, further business opportunities are unlikely to arise due to the prevailing uncertainty and the cautious approach of buyers influenced by the ongoing discussions about tariffs. PET: This week, PET prices were assessed steady in the US. Market activity in the US polyethylene terephthalate (PET) spot import market was heard to be subdued this week, as stakeholders continued to evaluate the effects of the recently announced tariff policy by US President Donald Trump. This policy, revealed on April 2, includes several exemptions, particularly for both recycled and virgin PET resin, which fall under the same harmonized tariff system code. The White House has indicated that HS codes 390761 and 390769 for PET are exempt from tariffs, offering some relief to market participants. Nevertheless, the ongoing assessment of these changes has led many players to remain cautious and reluctant to engage in significant market transactions.

data.title: Correction to India Has a Golden Opportunity to Capture U.S. Business From China Article on April 18
data.description:
By Tripti Lahiri

NEW DELHI — When President Trump opened his first trade war on China in 2018, a company called Zetwerk was just beginning to connect global customers with Indian suppliers of things like sheet metal and precision parts.

Today, it has a network of more than 10,000 suppliers and seven of its own electronics factories. Its latest facility, making parts for washing machines and other appliances, opened in March.

The first trade war helped India rise — and Trump's second one could be transformative, said Josh Foulger, the head of Zetwerk's electronics business.

"Is this India's moment?" said Foulger. "Yes."

With most Chinese exporters cut off for now from U.S. consumers by high tariffs, companies are looking for alternative places to produce and export to the U.S. — adding up to a golden opportunity for India.

That doesn't mean India will take its chance. The world's most populous nation has long trailed not just China but also smaller countries with nimbler governments. The number of people working in agriculture dwarfs the number employed in manufacturing.

Global high-tech firms and retailers say India is a harder place to do business than China or Vietnam, owing to government red tape, restive labor groups and an often-punitive approach to compliance and taxation. Vietnam, a country of 100 million people, exports $50 billion more in goods to the U.S. than India, whose population is 1.4 billion.

Indian officials in New Delhi are signaling they plan to be more open to Western businesses and are moving for a quick trade deal with the U.S. The country wants to emulate what has made China the world's unparalleled manufacturing powerhouse by offering not just manual assembly of goods but also design, parts and other knowhow.

"We are looking at building the entire value chain in India itself," said Ekroop Caur, the secretary for electronics and information technology in the southern state of Karnataka.

For the moment, most Indian goods face only the 10% tariff Trump has imposed globally, and certain exempted electronics such as iPhones have no tariff. The tariff on most Chinese goods is 145% while those electronics items are subject to a 20% rate.

Apple is already moving to export more iPhones to the U.S. from India, and the country currently accounts for about 20% of iPhone final production, according to market research estimates.

India's election last year, in which Prime Minister Narendra Modi's ruling Bharatiya Janata Party lost its outright majority, sent a clear message that many voters were dissatisfied with job prospects and low wages.

India now has a critical window for opening up with no local or national election on the horizon for about a year, said Tanvi Madan, an expert on India at the Brookings Institution.

The moment could be as important for India as the end of the Cold War, when it fell into a financial crisis caused by the collapse of the Soviet Union, then India's largest trading partner. In response, India opened up to foreign investment and cut a plethora of rules and regulations known as the "license raj," sparking faster growth.

"India has always worried about what opening up to the world will do to it," said Madan. "It should be thinking about what the world can do for it."

India's foreign minister, S. Jaishankar, said this month that the upending of global trade had "focused our own minds on the need for correcting what I would call a certain skewed nature of our openness to the global economy."

Still, companies find India remains prone to regulatory flip-flops and head-scratching interpretations of tax laws, leading to penalties or lengthy legal disputes.

India has demanded $1.4 billion for back taxes going back 12 years from a unit of German automaker Volkswagen, and an equivalent amount as a penalty, alleging it misclassified shipments imported into India to evade customs duties. The company denies wrongdoing, and has challenged the order in court.

"You think about China 30 years ago and Vietnam now, you have government officials in their localities waking up every day thinking, 'How can I make it easier for companies to come here and invest?'" said an executive with a U.S. firm long active in India.

Yet smartphones offer an example of what India can do when it puts its mind to it. A decade ago, when India started focusing on building phones, its annual mobile-phone exports were only about $250 million. Now the figure is more than $22 billion, with Apple accounting for about three-quarters of that.

A factory in Karnataka operated by Taiwan's Foxconn is coming on line this year and will eventually add annual production of 20 million phones, rivaling Foxconn's flagship plant in the neighboring state of Tamil Nadu.

Officials say a network of suppliers is growing up to feed the final assembly. New York state-based Corning, which has long made scratchproof glass for Apple phones, plans to start production in Tamil Nadu this year.

Companies say smartphones are benefiting from the government's attention and support, including manufacturing subsidies. India said this month it would extend the subsidies to phone components it now largely imports, and it has upgraded the freight terminal at Tamil Nadu's main airport to address bottlenecks that sometimes left vehicles carrying products waiting on the highway.

Foulger, the executive who connects global companies with Indian suppliers, was the country head for Foxconn in India before joining Zetwerk last year. He said the country still needed improvement on the most important quality for a global supply chain: consistency.

His mantra is to plan ahead, which he said he often repeats when he hears a supplier citing a string of public holidays as the reason for a delay or a worker blaming Bangalore's notorious traffic for being late.

"All of these things have to be like this 128-piece orchestra working in sync to make this happen," said Foulger. "It's going to be a journey, it's going to take some time, but because of India's inherent strengths I think we will prevail."

Write to Tripti Lahiri at tripti.lahiri@wsj.com

data.title: Buffered ETFs Protect Against Market Drops. They Are Selling Like Hotcakes.
data.description:
By Debbie Carlson

Buffered exchange-traded funds protect you against stock market losses, but you have to give up part of your upside in return.

The ETFs are selling strongly so far this year with the S&P 500 down about 9.8% on a total return basis. They appeal to retirees and other conservative investors.

According to FactSet, investors have sent nearly $7 billion into buffered ETFs as of early April. Also known as structured outcome or defined outcome ETFs, there are about 400 of these investment vehicles holding nearly $70 billion.

The products use options to buffer against stock market losses, anywhere from the first 10% to 100% downside protection, depending on the specific ETF strategy. They tend to have higher annual fees than most ETFs.

How They Work

The genesis of these ETFs was to keep investors in the market during normal pullbacks, says Lois Gregson, senior ETF analyst at FactSet.

Buffered ETFs put guardrails on an investor's return, using options to limit losses in exchange for capping gains. Generally, these strategies will buy put options against an index, which is the right to sell an asset at a certain price. To pay for the options, the strategy sells call options, the right to buy an asset, at certain levels above the market, limiting the upside. Typically, investors only receive the price return of the ETF, not any dividend.

The deeper the buffer, the tighter the upside cap. The $906 million FT Vest U.S. Equity Buffer February ETF provides a buffer against the first 10% of market losses and will match the S&P 500's price return up until 14.5%, minus fees and expenses. The $48 million FT Vest U.S. Equity Max Buffer February buffers against 100% of losses, but limits gains to 7.12%.

For ETFs with a 10% buffer, if the market is down, say, 5% at the end of the outcome period, the ETF won't have losses. If the market is down 15%, the ETF will be down 5%. If the market's return at the end of the outcome period is above the ETF's cap, the investor misses out on those gains.

These strategies allow investors exposure to equity growth, but without the full risk exposure, says Michael Loukas, CEO of TrueMark Investments, the firm behind the family of TrueShares ETFs.

"The point is, it gives these investors a much more enjoyable ride when they still need some growth. So they're willing to make that trade off of 'I'll take a little bit less growth if you can give me more downside protection or volatility management,'" he says.

Curtis Congdon, president of XML Financial Group, says he uses these ETFs for retirees or those approaching retirement and don't need current income. He says his retired clients generally don't mind if the ETFs' return lags behind the broader market's gains.

"If the market's up 20% and a retired client is up 15%, very rarely will they be upset that they didn't have all of the upside participation," he says, adding that he uses these as part of a broader, diversified portfolio, not as someone's only holding.

Fund Nuances

Although the ETFs are tradable, to receive the exact buffer and cap as marketed, investors must own them for the full outcome period. Typically the funds have a 12-month outcome period, such as Jan. 1 to Dec. 31, and many issuers launch new ones monthly.

Investors can buy and hold these ETFs, and the buffer resets based on whatever the market is trading at after the defined outcome period ends. So a 12-month ETF with a 10% buffer that ends on Dec. 31 will reset on Jan. 1.

Investors who don't buy an ETF on the first day of the defined investment period will need to refer to the fund issuer's website to understand potential outcome parameters. That's critical because if someone buys one of these ETFs in a down market, they may not have the full downside protection as marketed.

The net asset value of the fund will vary with the price of the underlying options position until it comes closer to the end of the outcome period, when the options contracts expire.

Loukas says many investors don't understand that these ETFs can fluctuate during the outcome period. "You won't realize that full buffer until the tail end of that investment period," he says.

Compared with plain-vanilla stock index ETFs, these are costly, says Nate Geraci, investment advisor and president of The ETF Store. The most expensive cost 0.95% annually, and some of the cheaper ones cost 0.5%. The average S&P 500 fund cost under 0.10%.

With a higher cost and no dividend payments, investors in buffered ETFs can lose money in a flat market, Geraci says.

These products are best suited for investors who make emotional decisions when markets swing, he says. Investors who can ride out volatile markets may be better off holding a diversified portfolio.

"You have different variations of buffer ETFs on the market, and you have to compare those to what an investor would receive if they were in a diversified portfolio of stocks and bonds," he says.

However, FactSet's Gregson says for investors who otherwise use annuities or structured notes to reduce risk, these ETFs are cheaper, more transparent and flexible. These ETFs are also cheaper, more tax efficient and convenient than buying individual options to hedge equity risk.

Write to editors@barrons.com
